title: Paul Clifford — Complete
author: Lytton, Edward Bulwer Lytton, Baron
summary: On stormy London streets a world of circumstance and consequence is traced: a sick woman raves in a low lodging while a dialect messenger, Dummie, scrambles for a Bible; landlady Mrs. Margery, a butcher, a cobbler, and other lowly figures populate the scene. The Prefaces explain the book’s purpose: to attack vicious prison discipline and a sanguinary penal code, to contrast fashionable and vulgar vice, and to plea for social reform. The narrative will follow a robber-hero (Paul Clifford) whose fate is set against that of William Brandon, exposing law, sympathy, and social causation.

characters.name: Dummie
characters.personality: loyal, plain-spoken, dialect-speaking errand-bearer; bumbling but earnest
characters.lifespan: appears tending the sick Judy in the lodging; fate not revealed in the provided excerpt

characters.name: Mrs. Margery
characters.lifespan: hosts the scene in Thames Court; fate not revealed in the provided excerpt

characters.name: Judy
characters.personality: sick, raving and near death in the lodging
characters.lifespan: described as raving and dying in the room; ultimate fate not further described in the excerpt

characters.name: Mother Bussblone
characters.personality: shopkeeperly, practical; a local source for goods
characters.lifespan: mentioned as a shop Dummie visited; fate not revealed in the provided excerpt

characters.name: Master Talkins
characters.personality: cobbler who preaches, sententious and sharp-tongued
characters.lifespan: refuses to supply a Bible to Dummie; fate not revealed in the excerpt

characters.name: The Sturdy Butcher
characters.personality: blunt, coarse, with dry humour (offers a book of plays instead of a Bible)
characters.lifespan: appears as a local tradesman; fate not revealed in the provided excerpt

characters.name: Paul Clifford
characters.personality: the novel’s robber-hero; presented in preface as morally complex, product of circumstance
characters.lifespan: portrayed as an outlaw whose destiny contrasts with William Brandon; in the preface he is indicated to face the law (at the convict's bar)

characters.name: William Brandon
characters.personality: honoured father, dispenser of the law; a foil to Paul Clifford
characters.lifespan: contrasted with Paul Clifford; portrayed in the preface as later sitting on the seat of justice opposite Clifford at the bar

characters.name: Crabbe
characters.lifespan: quoted at the book’s opening; not a dramatic character in the narrative

locations.name: Newgate (site of former Newgate Prison, central London)
Kenilworth (Kenelworth in the preface), Warwickshire, England

unusual_words.definition: relating to punishment or the legal system of penalties
unusual_words.context: Used in the preface criticizing 'penal institutions' and their defects.

unusual_words.definition: system of rules and instruction, here prison regulation
unusual_words.context: In the preface as 'prison-discipline' describing institutional practices.

unusual_words.definition: involving bloodshed or death; bloodthirsty
unusual_words.context: Preface criticizes a 'sanguinary criminal code' (i.e., severe capital punishments).

unusual_words.definition: a beginner or novice
unusual_words.context: Preface: the 'tyro learns from the felons in the prison-yard.'

unusual_words.definition: persons convicted of serious crimes
unusual_words.context: Used in preface about corrupting the boy by example from 'felons in the prison-yard.'

unusual_words.definition: the enclosed exercise area of a prison
unusual_words.context: Mentioned as the place where the novice learns crime by example.

unusual_words.definition: a crowd, often disorderly or violent; here the execution spectators
unusual_words.context: Preface: 'the mob gather round the drop at Newgate.'

unusual_words.definition: colloquial for the gallows trapdoor; place of execution
unusual_words.context: Appears in 'the drop at Newgate' describing the execution mechanism.

unusual_words.definition: historical prison in London known for executions
unusual_words.context: Referenced repeatedly as the site of execution and condemned cell.

unusual_words.definition: a wooden frame for executions by hanging
unusual_words.context: Used to describe the ultimate penalty dispensed by the penal code.

unusual_words.definition: literary work criticizing folly or vice through wit or irony
unusual_words.context: Preface: the book is described as a 'satire on the short cut' to the gallows.

unusual_words.definition: a distorted, exaggerated, or grotesque imitation
unusual_words.context: Preface explains 'a semi-burlesque or travesty in the earlier chapters.'

unusual_words.definition: partly comic parody or exaggerated imitation
unusual_words.context: Used in preface to describe tone of early chapters.

unusual_words.definition: hypocritical or sanctimonious talk; jargon of a social group
unusual_words.context: Preface contrasts the 'slang' of vulgar vice with the 'cant' of fashionable vice.

unusual_words.definition: title of supplementary essays attached to the edition
unusual_words.context: Preface mentions 'Supplementary Essays, entitled “Tomlinsoniana.”'

unusual_words.definition: consequences or deductions that follow logically
unusual_words.context: Tomlinsoniana contains 'corollaries to various problems suggested in the Novel.'

unusual_words.definition: concern for human welfare; charitable acts
unusual_words.context: Preface 1848 references conflicting feelings between 'philanthropy' and 'imagination.'

unusual_words.definition: conditions or factors that influence events or behavior
unusual_words.context: Preface emphasizes the 'Philosophy of Circumstance' shaping lives.

unusual_words.definition: public shame or disgrace
unusual_words.context: Preface: 'A child who is cradled in ignominy' is corrupted.

unusual_words.definition: made impure or corrupted
unusual_words.context: Preface: minds 'contaminated in infancy' by bad example.

unusual_words.definition: here used ironically for an institution of learning; 'the House of Correction' called an academy
unusual_words.context: Preface: 'his academy is the House of Correction.'

unusual_words.definition: moves around stealthily or restlessly, like a predator
unusual_words.context: Preface: 'it prowls near our homes' describing the metaphorical wild beast of corrupted youth.

unusual_words.definition: protection of oneself, sometimes by lethal means
unusual_words.context: Preface: society kills the beast 'in self-defence.'

unusual_words.definition: an older/rare form meaning sign or token in advance; presage
unusual_words.context: Preface uses 'a foresign of a coming change' to indicate literature prefiguring reform.

unusual_words.definition: unchangeable; fixed
unusual_words.context: Preface: 'immutable laws which proportion the rewards of labour.'

unusual_words.definition: exclusive rights or privileges
unusual_words.context: Preface: the author reflects on 'prerogatives... of Art.'

unusual_words.definition: branch of philosophy dealing with first principles and abstract matters
unusual_words.context: Preface: 'unsatisfactory as all metaphysics' in discussion of moral questions.

unusual_words.definition: more calm or clear; comparative of 'serene'
unusual_words.context: Preface: author seeks to 'look on the practical world through the serener one of the ideal.'

unusual_words.definition: intense feeling or passion; enthusiasm
unusual_words.context: Preface 1848: 'generous ardour of our early youth' motivating the novel.

unusual_words.definition: rest or sleep; tranquility
unusual_words.context: Opening poem: 'who press the downy couch' and the idea of repose is invoked.

unusual_words.definition: heavy flows of water; downpours
unusual_words.context: Chapter I: 'the rain fell in torrents' describing the stormy night.

unusual_words.definition: most remote, hidden, or unknown
unusual_words.context: Chapter I: 'Through one of the obscurest quarters of London' setting the scene.

unusual_words.definition: French for quarter or district; used here to indicate a neighborhood
unusual_words.context: Chapter I: 'appearance of the quartier in which they were situated.'

unusual_words.definition: an inn or tavern where ale is served
unusual_words.context: Chapter I: 'an inn or alehouse' describes the lodging used by Mrs. Margery.

unusual_words.definition: roundness of form; plumpness
unusual_words.context: Chapter I: landlady endowed 'with a comely rotundity of face and person.'

unusual_words.definition: an exclamation of contempt or dismissal
unusual_words.context: Mrs. Margery exclaims 'Pish, you fool!' when interrupting Dummie.

unusual_words.definition: dialectal form of 'will' (speaking accent)
unusual_words.context: Dummie's speech rendered in dialect: 'vy, it is no use desaving me. You knows you has only stepped...' includes 'vill'.

unusual_words.definition: dialectal spelling of 'service' (i.e., at your service)
unusual_words.context: Butcher says, 'it is quite at your sarvice!' in Dummie's dialectical account.

unusual_words.definition: dialectal form of 'deceiving' or 'misleading'
unusual_words.context: Mrs. Margery accuses Dummie of 'desaving' her when he pretends to have fetched the book.

unusual_words.definition: dialect for 'boozing-ken'—a tavern or drinking den
unusual_words.context: Dummie says he stepped 'from my boosing-ken to another.'

unusual_words.definition: dialect for 'went'
unusual_words.context: Dummie: 'I vent first to Mother Bussblone's'—dialectal narration of his errands.

unusual_words.definition: in this context likely street singers or troupers (dialect usage)
unusual_words.context: Dummie: 'chops the whiners morning and evening to the young ladies' describing Mother Bussblone's wares.

unusual_words.definition: dialectal form of 'asks'
unusual_words.context: Dummie says 'I axes there for a Bible', reflecting his spoken accent.

unusual_words.definition: dialect spelling of 'way'
unusual_words.context: Dummie recounts 'mayhap you'll be a getting it at Master Talkins'... 'over the vay.'

unusual_words.definition: dialectal preposition 'over' (used as 'hover the vay')
unusual_words.context: Dummie says 'So I goes hover the vay' in his dialectal explanation.

unusual_words.definition: title of a cheaply bound book; 'Halter' evokes hanging/gallows
unusual_words.context: Butcher offers 'a “Companion to the Halter,”' a darkly humorous substitute for a Bible.

unusual_words.definition: proper name; the cobbler character who also 'preaches'
unusual_words.context: Mentioned in Dummie's account as 'the cobbler as preaches.'

unusual_words.definition: a shoemaker; tradesman
unusual_words.context: Master Talkins is identified as a cobbler who also preaches.

unusual_words.definition: stitched or covered as a book
unusual_words.context: Butcher 'has a book of plays bound for all the world just like'un.'

unusual_words.definition: a small coin; here a cheap candle priced to match a farthing
unusual_words.context: The 'farthing candle' provides flickering light in the lodging.

unusual_words.definition: covered with soot; blackened by smoke

unusual_words.definition: small glass bottles, typically for medicine or chemicals

unusual_words.definition: having a split or break; damaged
unusual_words.context: A 'cracked cup' is noted among the room's sparse utensils.

unusual_words.definition: damaged or maimed; here furniture in poor repair

unusual_words.definition: comically or repulsively ugly or distorted
unusual_words.context: Figures and grotesque characters had been traced on the whitewashed walls.

unusual_words.definition: black; used poetically to describe color

unusual_words.definition: the personification of evil; the devil
unusual_words.context: Portraits of Satan 'such as he is accustomed to be drawn' decorate the walls.

unusual_words.definition: decorative details or additions

unusual_words.definition: stern or forbidding quality; gloom
unusual_words.context: The farthing candle 'gave a sort of grimness and menace' to the wall drawings.

unusual_words.definition: making a rapid succession of short, sharp noises

unusual_words.definition: stirring or disturbing vigorously
unusual_words.context: The gust was 'fiercely agitating the scanty flame of the lamps.'

unusual_words.definition: meager or insufficient in amount
unusual_words.context: The 'scanty flame of the lamps' struggled against darkness.

unusual_words.definition: light sources; street lamps
unusual_words.context: The lamps' scanty flame is described in the stormy night scene.

unusual_words.definition: a place populated by similar inhabitants; used pejoratively for a cluster of dwellings
unusual_words.context: Dummie soon came to 'a nest of low and dingy buildings.'

unusual_words.definition: gloomy and drab; dirty-looking
unusual_words.context: Describes the 'low and dingy buildings' of Thames Court.

unusual_words.definition: places frequently visited by particular people or creatures
unusual_words.context: The quarter contains 'haunts little loved by the gentlemen of the police.'

unusual_words.definition: going or traveling in a specified direction

unusual_words.definition: alone; isolated

unusual_words.definition: a request for information; seeking an item or fact

unusual_words.definition: matching or in agreement; here 'of a description correspondent with the appearance'
unusual_words.context: Shops were 'of a description correspondent with the appearance of the quartier.'

unusual_words.definition: expressing denial or refusal

unusual_words.definition: spoke quietly and indistinctly, often complainingly

unusual_words.definition: large or roomy
unusual_words.context: Dummie 'thrusting it into his ample pocket.'

unusual_words.definition: walked with long, decisive steps

unusual_words.definition: a sudden strong rush of wind

unusual_words.definition: burned brightly

unusual_words.definition: reddish in color, suggesting warmth

unusual_words.definition: the floor of a fireplace; used to mean home comfort

unusual_words.definition: allowed entry
unusual_words.context: Dummie 'was admitted by a lady' into the alehouse.

unusual_words.definition: pleasing in appearance; attractive
unusual_words.context: The landlady is described as 'a lady... endowed with a comely rotundity.'

unusual_words.definition: in an irritable or petulant manner
unusual_words.context: Mrs. Margery interrupts Dummie 'peevishly.'

unusual_words.definition: a person acting unwisely; here an insult
unusual_words.context: Mrs. Margery exclaims 'you fool!' while scolding Dummie.

unusual_words.definition: lack of seriousness or respect; frivolity
unusual_words.context: Preface criticizes 'the horrible levity with which the mob gather round the drop at Newgate.'

unusual_words.definition: travelers on a religious journey; here a title reference ('Pilgrims of the Rhine')
unusual_words.context: Preface lists author's earlier works including 'the Pilgrims of the Rhine.'

unusual_words.definition: title of another of the author's earlier works; used as a proper name
unusual_words.context: Preface references 'Rienzi' among previous fictions.

unusual_words.definition: ancient Roman city; referenced in the title 'Last Days of Pompeii'
unusual_words.context: Preface mentions 'the Last Days of Pompeii' as part of the author's oeuvre.

unusual_words.definition: a restatement of the meaning of a text in other words
unusual_words.context: Preface: 'the slang of the one circle is but an easy paraphrase of the cant of the other.'

unusual_words.definition: more elevated in style or imagination
unusual_words.context: Author notes he 'quitted loftier regions of imagination to trace' penal abuses.

unusual_words.definition: added to complete or enhance something
unusual_words.context: The preface states the 'Supplementary Essays' (Tomlinsoniana) have been restored to the edition.
